data.title: Confluent: A Fantastic 'Growth At A Reasonable Price' Investment
data.description:
Jerome Powell just said out loud what was on many of our minds: stocks appear to be quite expensive. And yet, taking this as an overarching blanket statement doesn't capture the true reality. While the S&P 500 is indeed sitting at stretched multiples, it's really the large-cap side of the house that is driving the gains, while small- and mid-cap growth stocks are still sitting at quite buyable levels. In my view, investors who are looking for downside protection should increasingly, by rotating, allocate away from large-cap and into small-cap.

Confluent, Inc. (NASDAQ:CFLT) is a great way to do so. The data streaming company, which helps its customers store their data and prepare it for use in cloud applications, has seen its share price decline by ~30% this year on worries over growth deceleration. And while Confluent may no longer be a growth star, its attractive combination of 20%+ revenue growth, rising operating margins, and cheap valuation multiples makes it a great rebound candidate.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Confluent in May, when the stock was still trading above $20 per share. Since then, Confluent has taken a fresh leg lower and missed out on the sharp rally in the S&P 500, which to me accentuates the "growth at a reasonable price" appeal of this stock. And despite the mounting pessimism here, Confluent raised its full-year expectations. Amid all of these factors, I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on this stock.

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on Confluent:

  • Substantial $100 billion TAM. Confluent believes that the global addressable market for data streaming and governance sits at over $100 billion, which gives the company ample room for expansion despite the broad set of players in the space (ranging from peers like Cloudera to large cloud giants like AWS).
  • Blue-chip customers. The company's strong reputation is affirmed by the blue-chip nature of its customer base, which includes some of the largest names in their respective industries, such as Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), Citigroup Inc. (C), Walmart Inc. (WMT), Square (SQ), and The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS).
  • High-margin recurring revenue. Subscription revenue makes up over 90% of Confluent's revenue base, stabilizing its business and providing for substantial opportunities to unlock economies of scale.
  • Making strides on operating margin. The company just crossed into positive operating margin territory last year, which is respectable for a company hitting over >20% y/y growth in subscription revenue.
  • Significant liquidity. Confluent has nearly $2 billion of cash on its balance sheet, giving the company plenty of firepower to chase growth opportunities.

The biggest draw for Confluent, however, is the stock's undemanding valuation. At current share prices just north of $19, Confluent trades at a market cap of $6.81 billion. Netting off the $1.94 billion of cash and $1.09 billion of convertible debt on Confluent's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $5.97 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Confluent has raised the low end of its subscription revenue guidance to $1.105 billion, from $1.10 billion previously. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting $1.15 billion in total revenue this year (which implies roughly flat services revenue of ~$42 million from the prior year), or 19% y/y growth.

For FY26, Street expectations go to $1.34 billion in revenue, or 16% y/y growth. This positions Confluent's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.2x EV/FY25 revenue.
  • 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue.

We should consider the fact that a number of other SaaS companies that have a high-teens/~20% growth profile trade at either high-single digit or low-teens multiples. Good examples are HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS), ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), AppFolio, Inc. (APPF), and Atlassian Corporation (TEAM):

In my view, we have a great opportunity to scoop up shares of Confluent while they're still trading at a very reasonable price relative to the company's own growth profile. Stay long here and use the recent dip to buy.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Confluent's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Confluent's revenue grew 20% y/y to $282.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $278.4 million (+18% y/y) by a two-point margin.

Now, it is true that Confluent's revenue has been decelerating, which is part of the reason that investors are pushing back on the stock. Subscription revenue growth of 21% y/y in Q2 decelerated 5 points from 26% growth in Q1.

The company attributed this deceleration to customers' growing desire to optimize IT budgets and rein in consumption and spending. Per CEO Jay Kreps' remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

I'd like to start by sharing some observations on our cloud business. In Q2, our larger customers continued their optimization efforts and adopted new use cases in a more measured pace. While we are confident that this elevated level of optimization will eventually subside, our outlook for the second half assumes consumption growth notably below what we've seen in the same period of prior years. Rohan will provide further details in his remarks.

Encouragingly, we've seen some customers commit to larger multiyear deals following the optimization efforts they undertook last year. This helped accelerate our RPO growth to 31% in the quarter, reflecting the deepening of our customer relationships as they plan for long-term growth. To accelerate use case expansions and support the long-term growth trajectory of our cloud business, we're driving operational enhancements across several areas in the business.

We do appreciate, however, the fact that conservatism and deceleration are already baked into Confluent's outlook (Q3 guidance calls for $281-$282 million in subscription revenue, or 17% growth at the midpoint, reflecting four points of further deceleration versus Q2 actuals)—which to me gives Confluent a chance to surprise to the upside.

Meanwhile, the company continues to land higher-value customers. As shown in the chart below, the company added 9 net-new customers who generate over $1 million in ARR, bringing the total to 219 such customers (+24% y/y).

We also appreciate the fact that despite compression in consumption trends, Confluent continues to boost its margin profile. Pro forma operating margins in Q2 rose 570bps y/y to 6.3%.

In my view, there is also substantial potential for Confluent to outperform its full-year ~6% pro forma operating margin target, considering it already hit these profitability levels in Q2 and can benefit from further efficiency measures.

Key takeaways

In my view, Confluent is a fantastic, overlooked stock. Even if growth decelerates to the high teens, the stock's ~5x forward revenue multiple sits two to three turns below most similar-growth peers, raising the likelihood of a rebound the next time the company gives the markets a beat-and-raise. With an expansive TAM and a growing trend to larger blue-chip clients, this is a secure "Buy."

data.title: Digital Realty Trust Analysis: Financial Health And Opportunity In Preferred Stock
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

In this article, we will take a closer look at Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (NYSE:DLR), its financial condition, and the characteristics of its preferred stock.

Company Overview

Digital Realty Trust, Inc., founded in 2004, provides a full range of data center, colocation, and connectivity solutions, serving leading global companies and service providers. As of the end of the second quarter of 2025, DLR had $48 billion in assets, over 300 data centers, 229000 cross-connects, and more than 5000 customers. The company's capital structure consists of 93% fixed-rate debt and 7% floating-rate debt. The company has an asset coverage ratio of 263% and generates a rental yield of around 11.58%. The asset yield is close to 12%. The current market capitalization is close to $60.5 billion. DLR has a credit rating by the major credit agencies: BBB+ credit rating by S&P, Baa2 by Moody's, and BBB by Fitch.

The common stock of the company is trading at $174.12, currently:

The forward P/FFO is 24.20, so the expected FFO yield comes to 4.13%.

DLR has 20 consecutive years of dividend payments and, for the past 12 months, has paid a $4.88 total dividend. The dividend yield is 2.80% Below, you can see the dividend history of the company:

Here's what the yield looks like over the past five years:

The company has close to 337.5 million shares outstanding, and with a $4.88 forward estimated dividend, it amounts to approximately $1.65 billion in common share distributions for the next four quarters. Here are the forward estimations:

Preferred stocks

All three preferred stocks of DLR are rated with a BBB- credit rating by S&P, BB+ by Fitch, and Baa3 by Moody's. All are traded below par at the time of writing.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.J) is trading at the moment at $22.67 price with a yield to worst of close to 5.79%. It pays a 5.25% cumulative distribution in the form of $0.328125 quarterly distributions, and $1.31 annual amount. DLR issued 8 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.J on 08/1/2017 for gross proceeds of $200 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 08/07/2022. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.L) is currently priced $22.24 and has a yield to worst of 5.85%. It pays a 5.20% cumulative distribution in the form of $0.325 quarterly distributions and $1.30 annual amount. DLR issued 12 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.L on 10/01/2019 for gross proceeds of $300 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 10/04/2024. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.K) is priced at $23.90 at the time of writing and has a yield of worst of 6.10%. The stock pays a 5.85% cumulative distribution per annum in the form of $0.365625 per quarter, and $1.46 annual amount. DLR issued 8 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.K on 03/04/2019 for gross proceeds of $250 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 03/15/2024. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

And here is a comparison between the preferred stocks of DLR and its peers in the REIT (real estate investment trust) sector, that trade below par and have high credit ratings:

We can see that DLR.PR.K outperforms all with a higher yield to worst.

DLR shares have many positives - such as high yields, stable dividends, and a credit rating. All shares are under par, which offers the potential for capital gains as the market normalizes and spreads narrow. In addition, DLR is a leader in data centers with >300 sites and >5000 customers, supported by AI and cloud search, which supports long-term stability. But we should also be on the lookout for risks when investing in them - such as sector risks or low liquidity. Preferred shares often have lower trading volume, which can make it difficult to sell quickly without losing money. Always check current market conditions before investing.

OTC bonds

DLR has unsecured bonds, too. We observe in the picture:

Their yields reach 5% and all are rated with a credit rating Baa2 by Moody's.

Conclusion

Digital Realty Trust, Inc. is establishing itself as a leader in the data center sector. The preferred shares are trading below par with high yields, outperforming their peers, and have the potential for capital gains as the market normalizes. However, low liquidity and sector risks require caution.

data.title: Bloomin' Brands May Have A Debt Problem
data.description:
Bloomin' Brands, Inc. (NASDAQ:BLMN) hasn’t had a great year. Its share price was $16.76 a year ago and has now fallen to $6.94 as of September 22, which is a 58 percent drop. Now the question is whether this restaurant holding company can turn itself around. On September 10, it announced that its Outback Steakhouse restaurant would introduce a $12.99 appetizer platter with three items, as well as a new color-changing cocktail that comes with a collectible platypus and platypus egg. It’s also introducing several other new menu items.

It appears that Outback is trying to do what the casual-dining chain Chili’s, which is owned by Brinker International, Inc. (EAT), has done. Chili’s introduced a popular $10.99 meal deal that turned its fortunes around and now several other casual-dining restaurants are introducing similar deals. I wrote about Brinker recently and while Chili’s is still posting great results, Brinker’s stock price has fallen since the article came out. Chili’s still seems like it will continue to perform well in my opinion, but investors are also concerned about the health of the restaurant sector in general. While Bloomin’s stock price has fallen significantly, in this environment it could be a value trap.

Outback is Bloomin Brands’ main restaurant concept, but its other concepts also have enough locations to impact its results. In its report for the second quarter of 2025, it said that it had a total of 1,118 restaurants in the United States. This included 678 Outback Steakhouse restaurants, so Outback made up 61 percent of its domestic holdings. There were also 208 Carrabba's restaurants, 166 Bonefish Grill restaurants, and 65 Fleming’s restaurants, as well as 1 Aussie Grill restaurant. Additionally, there are 343 Outback restaurants outside of the US, including 178 franchised restaurants in Brazil as well as 98 franchised restaurants in South Korea.

Bloomin’ reported mixed results for its restaurants in the second quarter. Outback Steakhouse’s comps were down 0.6 percent. Its Bonefish Grill restaurant reported -5.8 percent same-store sales growth. But comps were up 3.9 percent at Carrabba's and 3.8 percent at Fleming’s. In overall terms, same-store sales were down 0.1 percent, which doesn’t seem that bad. But some of Bloomin’s other financial metrics are more concerning.

Why This Is Not a Good Dividend Stock Right Now

The main appeal of Bloomin’ for the bulls is that it’s trading at a much lower valuation than other restaurants on several metrics. For example, after the market close on September 22 it had a forward P/S ratio of 0.15 versus 0.98 for the restaurant sector. It also had a forward P/E of 7.55 versus 19.81 for the sector, which is a 62 percent discount. But if you go by EV/EBITDA instead, Bloomin’ has an EV/EBITDA of 8.76 versus 10.95 for the median restaurant, which is a 20 percent discount.

The reason why Bloomin’ doesn’t look like as much of a deal based on EV/EBITDA is that it’s carrying a lot of debt, which increases its EV. This company currently has $2.17 billion in debt. This high debt load may be why it hasn’t bought back any stock in 2025, even though its stock price has fallen so much. And other metrics suggest that Bloomin’ could also be having a tough time making interest payments on its debt. Its levered free cash flow for the last twelve months is -$97.4 million.

The company has been trying to reduce its leverage recently. In December 2024, Bloomin’ sold a 67 percent stake in its Brazilian restaurants to the private equity firm Vinci Partners. Bloomin’ is still collecting installment payments from this transaction and is using them to pay off its debt. But its financial reports include the impact of these payments and the royalties from the Brazilian franchise, and even with this income, its levered free cash flow is still negative.

That might be why Seeking Alpha is currently displaying a warning that Bloomin’ is very likely to cut its dividend in the near future. The dividend is costing it too much. Bloomin’ already reduced its quarterly dividend from $0.24 last year to $0.15 earlier this year and has paid dividends of $0.15 per share for the past 3 quarters. But because its stock price has fallen so much recently, at a price of $6.94 per share, its annual dividend of $0.60 still produces an 8.6 percent forward yield, which is very high. Bloomin’ might also need to cut the dividend to pay for its Outback Steakhouse turnaround plan in my opinion, which will include some additional costs.

The Outback Steakhouse Turnaround Plan

Bloomin’ provided more details about its Outback Steakhouse turnaround plan during the second-quarter earnings call. The restaurant has now set up Ziosks at its tables. These kiosks allow guests to pay their checks themselves, which saves time for servers because they don’t have to pick up the checks. Chili’s first installed these devices in 2014 and was using them at 1,100 locations in 2024. So while this is a good step toward efficiency for Outback Steakhouse, it’s something that other restaurants have been doing for quite some time.

The restaurant has also decided to hire one server for every four tables at Outback. Previously, it had reduced the ratio to one server for every six tables under the assumption that giving the servers handheld devices would make them more efficient. But it appears that this hasn’t produced a satisfactory level of service quality. This initiative will increase Outback’s labor costs but it might be worth it if it makes guests happier.

Another turnaround initiative will be remodeling Outback Steakhouse restaurants. It’s unclear how much the remodels will cost because the company is currently testing three remodeling plans. What is clear is that Bloomin’ is focusing on remodeling its Outback restaurants before it adds any new locations. So Bloomin’s revenue isn’t going to rise in the short term because of new restaurants, and any short-term bull case for this company depends on increasing sales at the current Outback locations. Outback is a mature brand, though, so this isn’t a big surprise.

Bloomin’ is also making menu adjustments at Outback. The September 10 press release went into more detail about this initiative. New entrees at Outback include a sirloin and shrimp meal for $24.99, a salad with bacon and blue cheese for $5.99, and a brownie with ice cream for $10.79. There’s also a new chicken entree with bacon and honey mustard sauce for $21.49. And again, the biggest update is the $12.99 appetizer platter with fried onions, French fries, and fried cheese. So the menu updates could attract more value-oriented guests, which is important right now because consumers are trying to conserve their money.

Valuation Estimate for Bloomin’ Brands

If you take the midpoint of Bloomin’s EPS estimate for the full year of 2025, the company now expects adjusted EPS of $1.05. Its full-year 2024 EPS was $0.99 per share. That’s 6 percent EPS growth, which isn’t a lot, and it sounds like revenue and margins are likely to be flat next year. If you assume that no restaurants are added and comps drop by 0.1 percent in 2026, then revenue also drops 0.1 percent. That still rounds up to $1.05 per share.

Bloomin’ currently has 85 million shares outstanding so its projected earnings for 2026 would be $89 million. At a P/E ratio of 7.6, that would produce a market cap of $676 million. Divide that by 85 million shares, because buybacks are unlikely, and that is a share price of $7.95 per share. Again, Bloomin’s closing price on September 22 was $6.94 per share so its share price could rise by 15 percent over the next year if its valuation remains the same. But if the company cuts its dividend, its valuation is likely to fall.

While SA analysts currently rate Bloomin’ as a buy, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha’s quant score currently rate it as a hold. This company may have a low valuation that attracts value investors, but it also has a high debt load and a dividend that appears to be unsustainable, so I don’t think it’s as much of a bargain as it may appear to be.

Conclusion

While Bloomin’ has a plan to turn around Outback Steakhouse that might succeed, it’s too early to invest in the company for that reason. I believe it’s better to wait until other metrics, such as rising same-store sales, indicate that a turnaround is beginning. While it does look like Bloomin’s EPS could rise next year, it also appears that it may have to cut its dividend in my opinion. It did free up some cash by selling a stake in its Brazilian business, but in the long term, it needs to improve its operations to maintain the dividend at this level. So I would say that Bloomin’ is a hold.

data.title: Dow Stock: Crushed 56%, But Positioned For A Powerful Rebound
data.description:
Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW) stock has been crushed, losing more than 56% in the past year as high energy costs, slowing demand, and broader macroeconomic headwinds weighed on results. As one of the world’s largest chemical producers, Dow is highly cyclical making it vulnerable during downturns but also capable of sharp rebounds when market conditions improve. The key question for investors now: Do today’s depressed market conditions create a compelling long-term buying opportunity in Dow stock?

The Problems For Dow And The Dividend Cut

Dow’s most recent financial results show the challenging landscape for the giant chemical producer. Revenues of $10.23 billion missed estimates by $122 million and were 7.5% lower year-on-year while EBITDA tumbled from $1.4 billion to $745 million. The company has been and is facing a flurry of challenges. Most notably, the company’s end markets such as building materials and automotive have been sluggish and there is an associated pressure from oversupply on the market. This oversupply does not just come from lower demand but is further amplified by the actual supply component where China is oversupplying in key chemical and petrochemical segments. That pressures Dow’s margins.

Those margins had already been under pressure. The chemical industry is an energy-intensive industry and over the past years energy prices have soared. While oil prices have been under pressure which is a positive for naphtha, that only holds when the crack spreads also remain strong which generally is not the case as the macroeconomic outlook and thus demand have dimmed.

The company is one of the largest consumers of ethane as a feedstock for the production of ethylene, which Dow then processes into polyethylene, glycols, elastomers, styrene and PVC. That is perhaps where Dow feels the real pain as ethane is derived from liquid natural gas and gas prices are high. Particularly in Europe, the energy prices have been problematic for Dow. The company is postponing maintenance to save costs and has also shut down some parts of its plants. Dow has a production facility in Terneuzen, which happens to be my city of birth, and earlier this year the company postponed maintenance on one of its three crackers there. It really shows how pressing the input costs, competition and demand environment are for Dow. The complex in Terneuzen is the second largest that Dow has and the biggest outside of the U.S.

What I recall about Dow when I still lived close to Terneuzen is that whenever there would be large maintenance events there would be a huge inflow of additional staff and contractors in the area to get the crackers back in service as soon as possible. So, when Dow elects not to perform the required maintenance needed to keep the crackers in operation it speaks volumes about the challenges that Dow is facing.

So, we see that the company faces oversupply challenges, competitive pressures, high input costs including feedstock and high electricity costs and in The Netherlands, energy and emission policies and capacities make it extremely tough to produce in a competitive way.

The company has been on a cost-cutting spree for several years now to reduce operational costs, but it also had to slash its dividends by 50%.

Dow Closes European Facilities, Refinances Debt, And Identifies Over $6 Billion In Levers

Dow has identified around $6 billion in near-term levers. However, we note that most of these levers are non-recurring in nature. The company has around $3 billion in proceeds from Macquarie Asset Management expanding its equity stake in Diamond Infrastructure Solutions. In September, Dow announced that Macquarie had acquired an additional 9% stake in the partnership valued at $540 million. The other non-recurring items are a $250 million divestiture and $1.2 billion in proceeds from the NOVA judgement. That brings us to $4.25 billion. Capital expenditures for 2025 have been reduced by $1 billion and there is $1 billion in annualized cost reductions.

While the >$6 billion is promising, we do see that recurring cost cuts are relatively small. The company is shutting down 3 facilities in Europe starting in mid-2026 through the end of 2027. Additionally, the company has refinanced $1 billion in debt and extended its revolving credit facility until 2030. So, we see some one-offs, rationalization of the European footprint and streamlining debt maturities for the current tough market environment.

What Analysts Expect For Dow In The Third Quarter

Analysts are expecting Dow’s third quarter revenues to decline by 6.4% to $10.2 billion with earnings per share of -$0.29. Revenue growth on a quarterly basis is not expected until Q2 2026 when Dow is also expected to have positive earnings per share again. Full-year improvements are expected next year when Dow is also expected to be profitable on a full-year basis again, but the company is not expected to generate revenues in line with the $57.2 billion seen in 2022 for the foreseeable future and earnings per share are not expected to recover either.

Dow Stock Is Priced For The Bottom

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Dow is currently priced with 2025 earnings in mind and with that it is also priced for what is currently expected to be the bottom of the cycle. With the dividend cut, optimizations and proceeds from strategic partnerships and litigations coming in, I do believe that the company has quite a strong padding that also allows the company to substantially improve its leverage by 2027. So, I do believe that the stock is a Buy at this point.

We do note that earnings and free cash flow are not expected to reach the levels seen in 2022 for the foreseeable future. However, as the market has priced in the current market reality that also provides upside for recovery of earnings which is expected to start next year and provides a buy opportunity.

Conclusion: Dow Stock Price Has Been Aligned For The Current Market

I believe that Dow is currently priced fairly and that may provide a compelling entry point for the simple reason that on improving fundamentals in the years ahead the fair value for Dow stock will also substantially improve as will the dividend and that creates an opportunity to build an attractive yield on cost. The obvious risk to the bull thesis is that if feedstock costs remain high, competition remains fierce and macroeconomics remain challenging, Dow’s business will also be under continued pressure.

data.title: Moderna: Maintaining Sell Despite Consolidation - And Why MRNY Won't Help Either
data.description:
My Sell call on Moderna (MRNA) in April 2025 had looked a little bold at the time, given the extent of correction and perception of lack of room to correct further. So I will take a flat performance since then, while the S&P 500 has rallied by ~25% as an "I told you so" moment! Sticking to the fundamentals has shown its usefulness given Moderna's lackluster performance in comparison with the benchmark. The fundamental thesis is still a Sell, in fact vindicating a lot of my concerns.

Price action has been more benign than I expected though, in an absolute sense. The consolidation around the $25 levels tempted me to explore income alternatives like the YieldMax MRNA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MRNY). Because I don't see enough reasons to Buy Moderna, MRNY like option strategies often help when the underlying stock is consolidating with risks of corrections. As it turns out, MRNY's performance and structural deficiencies leave me with no confidence in MRNY as well. Hence the Conclusion - can't buy Moderna, can't trust MRNY. The best play around Moderna seems to be to avoid it altogether.

Falling Knife Like Fundamentals

My concerns around mRESVIA sales have been vindicated with "negligible" sales reported in Q2 2025. Approval in expanded age groups in the US could support a slacking sales trajectory here on paper. So could any news on international expansions. But the major issue is not with competitive pressures alone here. The overall TAM for RSV vaccines seems to be under pressure.

GSK's Arexvy has seen a large decline of ~40% YTD led by a demand decline in the US due to limited AICP recommendations since June 2024. Even Pfizer notes a lower vaccination rate due to softer ACIP recommendations. It does report a higher topline from Abrysvo, but that is because of newer use case in maternity indications and inorganic international expansion. Overall TAM concerns and competitive pressures will keep mRESVIA sales subdued. COVID vaccine uptick was not in the scheme of things in my earlier thesis, so the lack of turnaround there is not added pressure on Moderna's thesis.

Cash burn continues at a similar pace to the levels seen at the time of my earlier thesis. This does not come as a new negative surprise honestly, as work on the pipeline and R&D will need intense capital allocation. The net loss has shrunk to $825m in Q2, from ~$1b a quarter ago. The ~$5.1b in cash and short term investments corresponds to around 6-7 quarters of operating losses at today's net loss levels.

Management will be required to continue optimization of operational costs till the pipeline matures, which is years away. A cost sensitive environment along with a resource hungry pipeline (with binary outcomes) is not an ideal situation to be in. Moderna will be more a story around repurchases to keep investor sentiment as intact as possible, and cost optimization till the pipeline starts showing material progress.

So, the most critical aspect becomes the pipeline, where progress has been mixed. Some progress has been made in new asset additions like mRNA-2808, a mystery T-cell engager therapy entering Phase 1 trials. But no breakthrough developments to excite the markets have happened in the past few months. The risks are still intact around personalized manufacturing scalability and commercialization of the pipeline cancer solutions.

Resilient Prices

A known revenue drop phase, managed through expense optimization to keep net losses manageable, and no meaningful pathway to financial resurrection in the near term means that the share prices are going to react to market sentiment, particularly around the pipeline. Any progress that pushes Moderna towards commercialization could lead to sharp rallies - the corrections ensure that Moderna can soak up a lot of interest on any supportive news.

On the other hand share prices look to have low room for downside moves from the $25 levels it is hovering around now. The resilient share prices shows supportive strength in Moderna. I still rate Moderna a Sell because other than the technical resilience nothing has meaningfully taken shape to alter the core bearish thesis. I am willing to take a look at Moderna again when something materializes. In the interim, I am open to exploring a MRNY like option income strategy that could gain from a bearish but consolidating view I have on Moderna, till the thesis changes.

MRNY In Bad Times

Moderna has been through real stress scenarios in the past year or so. From around May 2024, Moderna is down by ~84%. That is some heavy correction territory. In the same period, which has spanned over 16 months now, MRNY has been able to save ~4 percentage points of the huge downside.

I have broken down the downside scenario into two phases of differing pace. The first phase is when Moderna crashes by ~49% in just over a 2 month period. This is a fast and sharp corrective movement that is usually hard for an option strategy to save much against the underlying. The option premium earned from the sold calls is usually ~1-3% depending on the IV levels prevalent. Before the second month of the crash, IV levels are not as elevated. So, the expected behavior is that during the first month, only ~1-3% of the premium earned is the drawdown saved by the option strategy. In the subsequent months, if the correction and volatility levels are still elevated, then the savings or alpha from the option strategy can improve and reach even 3-5%, depending on how high the volatility levels are. MRNY saves overall 4 percentage points over Moderna- which is quite moderate to low in the light of the expected and observed better performance in the second month of the crash. Given the extent of the crash, I would have expected the IV to be much higher and that leading to 3-5 percentage points gains in MRNY adding to a 1-3 percentage point gain in the first month. MRNY captures the lowest end of the spectrum here.

The first phase discussed above clearly shows how MRNY's passive monthly strategy means that the call positions are still kept intact even if the premiums have completely eroded in no time. An active strategy may have rolled out the strike price and optimized gains, rolling down strikes as they became worthless. MRNY loses out in this regime due to a passive approach.

The second phase of correction is from August 2024 and lasts about 8 months. This phase shows how MRNY performs in a more steadier correction regime. In such regimes, I expect MRNY to gain 1-3 percentage points each month. This is actually a very fair expectation if you look at the premium specifics. Even today, when MRNA is consolidating after a drop in volatility levels, the ATM $26 strike call option expiring in the end of October has a premium trading at ~10% of the stock price.

If there are a minimum of 10 percentage points at stake every month, even disregarding the higher volatility levels seen earlier, one would expect MRNY to return a bare minimum of 8 percentage points of cumulative outperformance in 8 months. MRNY's implementation and intra month volatile moves shows how in reality, the capture premium leads to only a 5 percentage point drawdown reduction in a slower grind. So, putting both the scenarios together, in sharp falls, either fast paced or a slower grind, MRNY hardly smoothens the drawdown materially.

In Flatter Markets

If not great drawdown management, an option strategy sometimes does well in smaller corrections and flat markets. We will check MRNY's performance in those conditions in this section. The period I have taken to analyze this behavior is from April 2025 to now. In this period, Moderna has been up and down with periods of up to 30% upside and 5-10% downsides. It has been still volatile, but the volatility levels have come down from what it was during the ~85% correction from 2023. The end of end change in Moderna share prices is a less than 4%, what can be called broadly flat. In this flat period, supposedly MRNY's home run, MRNY has returned even less than Moderna's 4%.

Going by the premiums trading at around 10% of the stock price, as seen earlier, I would have expected at least a percentage point of outperformance in MRNY's total returns over Moderna's in this flat period, but that is hardly visible. Those who argue that the flat period analyzed here has had its fair share of ups and downs should note that most stocks will never be ideally flat. Often flat periods are a collection of mini crashes and rallies, and in realistic market scenarios MRNY can be said to have failed to deliver the promised alpha from the option layer.

If Moderna Rebounds

The bull territory is not an option strategy's strength, but upside caps coming into play. In a close to ~93% upmove over 3 months in 2024, MRNY was able to trace only 43% total returns, leaving so much of the upside uncaptured. This is particularly an issue in a sharp rally.

A more recent ~33% rally in over 3 months is relatively better captured, thanks to the higher premiums of the options written.

No Case for MRNY

Overall, the expected behavior from MRNY is a function of three factors. One, the pitfalls buywrite option strategies in general that MRNY starts out with. Despite the false start, a few option strategies do end up doing relatively well, particularly if their volatility profiles are suitable for option writing. Generally, single stock option buywrites with evergreen yield generating potential are near non existent. Some of the basket of portfolio approaches like YMAX, do well enough to qualify as a Buy.

So, the second factor is the underlying's fit with the strategy. In the case of Moderna, this box seems to be ticked, with Moderna's volatility being high enough to generate ~10% premiums for a monthly ATM call option. It is sufficiently high without being too high to kill the strategy, like seen in the case of MSTY which suffers due to extreme volatility of the underlying MSTR position.

The third factor that may be dragging MRNY's performance is the implementation specifics. Despite being passive, calls of exact strike process, expiry dates and even minor rounding off when it comes to portfolio coverage through option writing, could lead to performance issues.

Whatever the underlying reasons, investors should expect the following behavior from MRNY. In bull markets, it will trace Moderna for decent rallies of up to ~10% a month, say (which is the extent of premium gained every month). This is decent upside capture actually. But the let down is where MRNY is expected to do well. MRNY barely captures a single percentage point of the ~10% premiums monthly even in correcting or flat markets, which makes its drawdown or flat market outperformance insignificant compared to upside losses in sharp rallies.

I am also wary of the aggressive yields in MRNY. Investors may find the TTM yield of ~150% mind boggling and misleadingly profitable. But this is because of structural NAV erosion, which inflates the yield on today's lower base for MRNY prices. On a cost basis, the yield is only ~13% today and likely to come down exponentially over time as it has in the past.

MRNY is clearly not an all weather option strategy because it will almost digest the full extent of a drawdown while not capturing sharp upsides. For tactical uses, the current Sell outlook on MRNY means we should focus on its drawdown mitigation, which is not much for the upside lost in case of a rebound. I see Moderna mostly flat or slightly down with limited downside risk of say 20-30%, given the extent of corrections seen already and the strong consolidation in the past 6 months. In these conditions, MRNY will only save investors a 4-5 percentage point drawdown over the next couple of quarters if prices correct or stagnate. MRNY, in short does nothing better than holding Moderna directly and even generating income from it. So, I think it is best rated a Sell. The expense ratio of ~1.27% is too high to end up with something with little to gain and much to lose over simply owning Moderna (which is itself a Hold at best).

data.title: Opportunities In The Insurance Sector (Part 5): A Look At Lincoln National And Its Preferred Stock
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue our series on opportunities in the insurance sector with Lincoln National Corporation (NYSE:LNC) and its preferred stock (NYSE:LNC.PR.D).

Company Overview

Lincoln National Corporation was founded in 1905 and is headquartered in Radnor, Pennsylvania. It is a holding company and part of the 200 largest US corporations. As of the end of the second quarter of 2025, LNC had assets of about $399 billion, total debt of $9.12 billion, and equity of $9.5 billion. The market capitalization is close to $7.7 billion. The debt-to-asset ratio is about 2.28%, and the equity-to-debt ratio is 1.04, which is favorable for creditors. The common stock of the company is now traded at $40.55.

Fitch rated LNC's Long-Term Issuer Default Rating at "A-" and senior unsecured debt rating at "BBB+". The company also has a "Baa2" credit rating by Moody's and "BBB+" by S&P:

In the chart below, we can see LNC outperforms SPDR® S&P Insurance ETF (KIE) in 1-year total return:

LNC has had 35 consecutive years of dividend payments. The total dividend for the last 12 months is $1.80, and the dividend yield is 4.44%. The dividend and yield history is presented below:

The 5-year dividend yield chart:

With close to 171 million common shares outstanding, and with a $1.80 forward estimated dividend, LNC amounts to approximately $308 million in common share distributions for the next year from now. You can see the forward estimations below:

The preferred stock

(LNC.PR.D) is trading at $26.54, with a yield to worst of 6.40%. The preferred stock pays non-cumulative distributions of 9% through quarterly distributions of $0.5625, or $2.25 annually. It was issued on 11/15/2022 with 20 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $500 million in gross proceeds. It is rated Ba1 by Moody’s, BBB- by Fitch, and BBB- by S&P, and has 2.19 years to call, with a call date of 12/01/2027.

Compared to the other peers, preferred stocks in the insurance sector, LNC.PR.D, stand at the top with yield to worst, as you can see in the picture:

Over-the-counter

You can see the LNC's unsecured bonds here:

These bonds have Moody's ratings of A2 to Ba1, even a credit rating like the higher-yielding preferred stock. Their yield range is 4 to 6%. LNC also has another preferred stock - perpetual, with a 9.25% coupon, which has the same Moody's credit rating of "Ba1". However, this stock has a maturity date of 03/01/2028 and a yield to maturity of close to 5.1%. The 130 basis point difference in yield to maturity between it and LNC-D (6.4%) is inexplicable, and if yields were equal, the price of LNC-D would have to rise above $27.20.

Conclusion

LNC has established itself as a leading insurance company with over 120 years of history. With assets of approximately $399 billion, a low debt-to-assets ratio, and a favorable equity-to-debt ratio, LNC has demonstrated financial strength, as supported by its credit ratings. LNC.PR.D offers a YTW of 6.40% at a price of $26.54 and an annual dividend of $2.25, which positions it among the best in the insurance sector compared to its peers, but investors should still consider potential yield discrepancies and monitor market conditions before the redemption of LNC.PR.D in December 2027.

data.title: Hyatt Hotels: Still Years Of Asset-Light Earnings Growth Ahead
data.description:
It's been almost three years since my last piece on global hotelier Hyatt Hotels (NYSE:H). Rating it a "Buy," I was particularly bullish on Hyatt's long-term growth prospects, given its industry-leading pipeline and increasingly fee-based, asset-light business model. While the shares have done OK since then, returning around 50%, their recent performance has been lackluster. Like its peers, Hyatt has trailed the wider market this year amid signs of weakening consumer demand in the United States, and its shares are down around 11% so far (including dividends).

That drop provides a nice opportunity to revisit the investment case here. Although there are signs of cyclical softness affecting its business, Hyatt's long-term growth drivers otherwise look healthy. I remain bullish.

Business Model Recap

Like many other global hotel firms, Hyatt is really more of a fee-based business than an owner-operator. In fact, only around 4% of its rooms were in properties that were owned or leased by the company itself at the end of last quarter. In contrast, nearly 60% were in hotels operating under a management agreement, while the rest were in franchised hotels.

The difference between franchised and managed hotels is subtle. Under a typical franchise agreement, Hyatt will simply receive a cut of the hotel's room revenue. Under a managed contract, it typically receives a base fee - which is also a cut of room revenue - and a share of the hotel's earnings. As the name suggests, Hyatt will be responsible for the management of these properties, but these costs are reimbursed by the owners. Both franchised and managed hotels are asset light (owners are responsible for capital expenditures), but the latter tends to be somewhat more cyclical since hotel-level profitability is more volatile than room revenue.

The upshot of the above is that Hyatt's long-term fee and earnings growth will largely be driven by two things. The first is the total number of rooms in its system. The second is the revenue per available room ('RevPAR'). In turn, RevPAR is a function of a hotel's occupancy ratio and the average daily room rate.

With the above in mind, my bullish case for Hyatt was fairly simple: grow RevPAR by a GDP-like 3%-4% and net rooms by 2-3 points higher than that, leading to double-digit annualized earnings growth assuming some profit margin expansion over time.

Signs Of Cyclical Softness, But Long-Term Growth Drivers Still In Place

RevPAR growth is currently running below this trend, clocking in at 1.6% last quarter. Like many consumer-facing companies, Hyatt appears to have seen economic uncertainty translate into tepid demand, particularly in the United States, where RevPAR fell 0.1% last quarter on a 0.9% drop in the occupancy ratio. As a result, management now expects full-year firm-wide RevPAR growth to be between 1% and 3%. This is down from 2%-4% at the start of the year.

First and foremost, I should point out that this is not a Hyatt-specific issue. Peers like Hilton (HLT) and Marriott (MAR) have also downgraded 2025 RevPAR guidance following a weak H1.

More important than that, Hyatt's other long-term growth drivers still look healthy. For instance, while its near-term RevPAR outlook is a little soft, unit growth remains robust. Hyatt reported around 364,000 rooms in its system last quarter, up 6.5% year-on-year excluding the impact of acquisitions.

As a result, fee growth also remains quite strong. Hyatt generated $573 million in net fees in the first half of 2025, around 13% higher than the year-ago period. Similarly, the firm's management and franchising segment contributed $474 million to its adjusted EBITDA in H1 2025, up around 12% year-on-year.

Hyatt can sustain strong unit growth for the foreseeable future. It has around 140,000 rooms in its pipeline, up roughly 8% year-over-year and over 20% since my last piece. As a result, the size of its pipeline has largely kept up with its system growth, remaining broadly stable at around 40% of its existing rooms. Not only is that relatively large compared to peers, but it can sustain a solid rate of annual growth for the foreseeable future, even if the rate of new pipeline signings were to slow down.

It is important to mention some of the reasons why third-party owners are keen to partner with branded players like Hyatt. One is the fact that branded hotels typically enjoy above-average occupancy rates. This, in turn, is partly explained by their loyalty programs. Hyatt's is in good health. It had 58 million members at the end of last quarter, up over 20% year-on-year. That may look small next to peers (Hilton's loyalty program has 226 million members and Marriott's has nearly 250 million), but per hotel, Hyatt's program actually has significantly more members. That suggests to me that the firm still has plenty of untapped growth potential.

Portfolio Reshuffling To Impact Near-Term Earnings

Hyatt's transition to an asset-light model naturally involves the disposal of company-owned/leased properties. As these owner-operator earnings fall off the books, it can have a short-term impact on firm-wide earnings and comps, as shown in a breakdown of Hyatt's year-on-year EBITDA performance.

In addition to this, Hyatt recently completed its $2.6 billion acquisition of Playa Hotels & Resorts. Soon after, the company announced that it was offloading the real estate for $2 billion, with Hyatt signing long-term management contracts for these properties. This is expected to generate around $60-65 million in annual EBITDA by 2027, implying a cheap valuation given a net outlay of just $600 million. Among other things, this has caused Hyatt's interest bill to increase this year, given it has taken on additional debt to finance the initial acquisition (debt will be paid back down with the cash generated by the asset sale).

The Valuation

The reason I mention the above is to provide some context for the stock's current valuation. Not only does it appear to trade for a rich-looking 59x consensus 2025 EPS, but consensus also implies a steep year-on-year decline in earnings. This swing is just a function of the above. As you can see, analysts expect EPS to rebound again sharply in 2026, before continuing to grow at a strong double-digit cadence thereafter.

There is another driver of Hyatt's EPS growth that I haven't yet mentioned, which is that the company's increasingly asset-light business model does not require high levels of investment to grow. Indeed, Hyatt is only guiding for $150 million in capital expenditures this year. Despite strong room count growth, this is $20 million less than it spent in 2024.

This fundamental aspect of the fee business allows Hyatt to spend heavily on capital returns to shareholders instead, mostly via share repurchases. In fact, Hyatt has reduced shares outstanding by over 10% since I last covered it. On top of the solid growth coming from the underlying business, this buyback component will provide an additional boost to long-term EPS growth.

Their asset-light business models and solid underlying growth prospects help explain why the global hotel stocks typically command rich valuations. Per Seeking Alpha, Hilton, Marriott, and InterContinental Hotels (IHG) are trading for between 21x and 33x consensus EPS.

Hyatt's stock currently trades for around $139. Based on current consensus, its P/E multiple could de-rate to around 20x over the next five years or so, and investors would still be in line for a ~10% annualized return. Accordingly, I would be inclined to view this year's weak share price performance as an opportunity to invest. On that basis, I reiterate my initial "Buy" rating for Hyatt.

data.title: Building A Current Growth And Income Portfolio For My Daughter
data.description:
As a now retired income investor, I mostly enjoy researching and writing about passive income investments. But my investing journey began with growth stocks, and then a combination of growth and income as my portfolio balance grew. I did not start this process until I was in my 50s after going through a troubling personal time that caused my net worth to reset to zero due to a bankruptcy.

Now that I am retired and embarking on a new chapter in my life, I am looking at setting up an investment portfolio for my 29-year-old daughter who is currently in graduate school. I decided that I want to support her financially by creating a long-term growth and income portfolio that will build its value over time by capturing appreciation from small cap growth stocks while also generating recurring distributions from income investments that can be set to auto-reinvest. That will allow those funds to grow over time by compounding the distributions and continually add more shares.

I plan to create a portfolio with ten holdings initially, and roughly $5,000 invested in each stock or ETF. My plan is to buy shares of 5 growth stocks/ETFs that pay no dividends (or very small dividends) and 5 ETFs that generate recurring passive income on a monthly or weekly basis. I am building this portfolio as a sort of “test portfolio” in my Fidelity brokerage account and intend to monitor the performance and come back here and report the results after a year, depending on what happens in the meantime.

4 Growth Stocks and 1 Growth ETF

The growth-oriented securities that I will be including, along with their market sector and industry:

  • American Superconductor Corporation (AMSC) – Industrials/Electrical Components and Equipment.
  • Tutor Perini Corporation (TPC) – Industrials/Construction and Engineering.
  • Pagaya Technologies Ltd (PGY) – Technology/Application Software (Fintech).
  • Robinhood (HOOD) – Financials/Investment Banking and Brokerage.
  • Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM) – Quantum Computing growth ETF.

5 High-Yield Income ETFs

There are five income ETFs that I intend to include:

  • Roundhill Ether Covered Call Strategy ETF (YETH) – weekly covered call income from Ether crypto.
  • GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (GGN) – Gold and Energy income fund.
  • Amplify CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (IDVO) – international income fund.
  • Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) – covered call income from Nasdaq 100.
  • SPDR® Blackstone Senior Loan ETF (SRLN) – senior loan income fund.

Portfolio Strategy

This portfolio will take advantage of current and expected future trends like the Fourth Industrial Revolution, Cryptocurrency, Quantum Computing, Infrastructure Buildout, AI and Data Center Growth, Fintech solutions, International stocks, and general macroeconomic trends that are driving markets higher over the next ten to twenty years or more. These are mostly emerging trends that are gaining traction and have momentum heading into the end of 2025.

As you can see from the different market sectors represented by this portfolio mix, I am also attempting to diversify. I do not want my funds’ top holdings to all include Nvidia (NVDA), for example. The proposed mix above only has one fund, GPIQ, that is heavily AI/tech dependent with NVDA as a top holding.

I also have the BIG investments now recommended by Blackrock: Bitcoin, although I am going with ether (ETH-USD) instead and HOOD, which benefits from BTC continuing to rise in price; International, I have IDVO, and Gold, this is where GGN comes into play. Blackrock also recommends growth stocks that are benefiting from recent trends and events such as lower interest rates.

According to BlackRock, the rate cut strengthens the investment case for large-cap growth stocks, international equities, and alternative assets such as gold (CUR) and bitcoin (BTC-USD). The firm highlighted that lower borrowing costs could support corporate earnings and valuations, particularly for growth-oriented companies.

I will attempt to manage the portfolio by setting the income paying funds to automatically reinvest each week in the case of YETH, and each month in the case of SRLN, GGN, IDVO, and GPIQ. The growth stocks will be rebalanced monthly by trimming shares to capture gains and/or by adding shares at lower prices during market corrections. I will keep a cash balance available for dry powder to use when those market opportunities arise.

The growth stocks will be purchased as the opportunities arise during whatever market corrections we experience in the coming weeks or when prices of the individual stocks drop down to my Buy targets. For example, I will look to buy AMSC under $60.

In the section below I will discuss my rationale for purchasing these particular growth stocks, each of which deserves its own entire full-length article. Instead, I will include a brief summary of why I believe these particular growth stocks and one growth-oriented ETF, QTUM, deserve a spot in my daughter’s portfolio.

I will not go into detail in this article regarding the income-oriented ETFs and will leave that for readers to research on their own until I find the time to write a second article describing my rationale for choosing those particular ETFs. Suffice to say that for now, I am looking to build a diversified portfolio based on uncorrelated asset classes like gold, crypto, energy, infrastructure, technology, international stocks, and financial companies. Some are likely to move up in price, while others may move down, depending on what is happening with the world economy and geopolitics, which both change radically from day to day lately.

Rationale for Growth Stocks

Following is a brief summary of each pick for the growth stocks and ETF in the portfolio based on emerging trends and strong growth potential that I am seeing in the market today. Keep in mind that the overall market appears to be reaching overbought territory and may be ripe for a correction, so some of these picks may see a short-term price decline if there is a market correction in the next few weeks.

AMSC

I last wrote about AMSC in November 2024, when I rated the stock a Strong Buy. This was what I said at the time:

With a market cap of only $950M and relatively light trading volume along with 7% short interest, I expect to see some price volatility. It would not surprise me to see the stock soar well above the $25 mark after investors have a chance to listen to the earnings call.

Prior to the Q2 2024 AMSC earnings report, the consensus revenue estimates were raised 3 times and show promising growth for the next 3 years. The Q2 report suggests that even higher revisions are now likely to be issued based on the outlook.

Since that article was published the stock price has risen 150%.

Now that AMSC has reported Q1 2025 earnings, the potential for continuing exceptional growth is being realized as the company sees continued strong demand from the semiconductors and materials sectors. Revenues reported in Q125 were 80% above the year ago quarter and the company achieved expanded gross margins above 30%.

AMSC is well positioned to leverage the key drivers that are propelling the company’s future growth prospects. These are all global trends that are not going away overnight, including expanding fab capacity for semiconductor production, global investments in EVs and clean power, voltage management solutions for traditional power grids, and growing needs for enhanced grid stability from AI and data center demands. Rising geopolitical tensions are also paving the way for additional orders from the military including ship protection systems for both the U.S. and Royal Canadian Navy.

TPC

Tutor Perini is in the construction and design build industry. It operates as a contractor in three business segments: Civil, Building, and Specialty Contractor services. The stock is up 150% in the past year and shows solid indications of continuing growth with Strong Buy ratings from Wall St, SA analysts, and the SA Quant system ranks TPC #1 out of 36 companies in the Construction and Engineering industry. The latest

On the latest earnings call, TPC reported record revenues, operating income, and backlog and raised guidance for the remainder of 2025. According to CEO Gary Smalley, the record backlog should support future double-digit growth:

To briefly recap, we delivered extraordinary results for the second quarter that once again exceeded our expectations with record operating cash flow and continued strong revenue, operating income and earnings growth and with backlog that has doubled year-over-year to a new all-time record of $21.1 billion. This record backlog should enable us to generate double- digit revenue growth and strong earnings for the foreseeable future, while also serving as a catalyst for continued strong cash flow as the newer projects progress through design and into construction.

TPC has also risen in price by more than 150% in the past year yet still trades at a reasonable price at about 17x FY2026 earnings. Earnings revisions keep getting revised upwards by the 3 Wall Street analysts who provide estimates and those analysts currently expect the company to deliver $6.00 in EPS by December 2027.

PGY

Pagaya Technologies is in the fintech, or financial software technology, business. According to the SA company profile:

The company develops and implements proprietary artificial intelligence technology and related software solutions to assist partners to originate loans and other assets.

The stock price is up over 200% in the past year yet remains a bargain under $40, which is less than 15x forward earnings.

Meanwhile, on the Q2 2025 earnings report, the company raised 2025 revenue guidance and reported record results across all key metrics. The SA Quant system ranks PGY #1 out of 181 Application Software companies. Wall Street analysts also rate the stock a Strong Buy.

There are 7 Wall Street analysts following PGY stock and they estimate a forward P/E of 12.5x based on FY2026 earnings.

HOOD

Although HOOD stock is up over 450% in the past year, I still feel that it has plenty of tailwinds to support future growth and potential for additional capital appreciation. In addition to being included in the S&P 500 index, the market cap now exceeds $110B.

In addition, Robinhood recently announced a new social media app that should help propel the stock to new heights, at least according to one analyst from Bank of America:

Bank of America Securities on Thursday reiterated its Buy rating on Robinhood Markets as analyst Craig Siegenthaler praised some of the product launches the Vlad Tenev-run fintech recently announced. He particularly cheered Robinhood's new social media platform that will let users post their trades, discuss strategies, track traders' performance, and follow hedge funds, politicians and insiders, calling the network a "game changer."

I view Robinhood as a disruptive player in the venerable but aging cast of brokers with an advantage of attracting younger investors by offering unique advantages like this new social media app and crypto trading on its platform. You don’t see Vanguard or E-Trade, now part of Morgan Stanley, offering such novel ideas to attract young investors. In fact, E-Trade just announced that crypto trading will be available “next year,” as they try to play catch-up with Robinhood.

The quarterly EPS is growing and upward revisions are pouring in with 16 upward revisions for the next quarter and 0 downward revisions.

The valuation is a bit of a concern and the stock price may retreat from its all-time high in the next market correction, however, I believe that the company has a good business model that will continue to attract a new base of young investors who will support long-term growth.

QTUM

Another emerging trend which I have written about on several occasions includes the powerful new computing paradigm known as “quantum computing”, not to be confused with the stock that has the same name. There are several distinct players who are making waves in QC including IonQ, Inc (IONQ) and Rigetti Computing (RGTI). I last covered RGTI in November 2024 when I rated the stock a Hold after Q324 earnings but suggested putting them on a watch list. The stock has soared since that article was published.

Other players in the QC field include D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) and other smaller entrants who may have something to offer in the future. Because the technology is still in the mostly research and development phase with many companies striving for the “quantum advantage”, I prefer to play in this space by acquiring shares of QTUM, an ETF that holds many of the biggest players in QC. Some of those bigger players also include Oracle, Microsoft, and Alphabet (GOOG).

When I reviewed QTUM in December 2024, my article was an “Editor’s Pick” and I stated at the time that I believed Quantum Computing would be the “next big thing” in technology. The ETF delivered a total return of 37% in the 9 months since that article was written but still has plenty of upside in my opinion as the technology matures.

Summary: Positioned for Emerging Growth Opportunities

This portfolio is designed for someone who is just starting their career and has plenty of time in the market to capture gains from growth stocks, while generating passive income to reinvest for additional portfolio growth. The growth stocks and ETFs chosen for this portfolio represent a variety of asset classes and market sectors and are intended to capitalize on the market trends and macroeconomic environment that we have today.

Thirty years ago, it was the dawn of the Internet and cloud computing, and companies like Microsoft, Google, and Apple were just getting started. This portfolio is designed to capture the future companies that are most likely to grow and offer this young investor an opportunity to generate wealth that will hopefully help support her eventual retirement knowing that Social Security and other “safety nets” may no longer exist by then.

I will monitor the portfolio and discuss the details with my daughter so that she understands what she is invested in, and make changes as appropriate as time goes on. I also plan to revisit this portfolio on Seeking Alpha and review the performance after a year goes by. Stay tuned.

data.title: Hudson Pacific Properties: West Coast Office Vacancies Remain Elevated
data.description:
Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc.'s (NYSE:HPP) preferreds might be deeply discounted, but an occupancy rate on the rise and set to move higher in response to an immense wall of upcoming lease expirations renders a position in them as too high-risk. The West Coast office-focused REIT became a poster boy for the long-term negative ramifications of the pandemic on demand for office space. It has seen its commons and preferreds stage a tepid recovery from 52-week lows, with the latter less risky against following a June equity offering that allowed the REIT to raise a material $690 million gross. HPP sold warrants and 197.2 million common shares at a price of $2.23 per share. This was at a premium to its share price before the news of the upcoming offering was published, with Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS) acting as a cornerstone investor with $300 million invested on behalf of its clients to take up 43% of HPP’s offering.

The offering was a strong vote of confidence in HPP's Class A office portfolio, which saw 72 new and renewal leases totaling 558,055 square feet signed during the fiscal 2025 second quarter. Around 1.2 million square feet of leases have been signed through the first two quarters of 2025, with HPP placing its leasing pipeline at 2 million square feet. Crucially, this drove a positive GAAP rent spread of 4.9%, albeit with cash rent spreads dipping by 1.8% year-over-year. Focus moves to HPP's heavily front-loaded lease maturity ladder. This will see leases representing 44.6% of HPP's office annualized base rent expiring over the next three fiscal years to the end of 2028.

The material wall of lease expirations provides HPP with a Sisyphean task to lease the spaces, especially with an in-service office occupancy rate that stood at 75.1% as of the end of the second quarter, with the leased rate higher at 76.2%. These figures were respectively down from their year-ago comp of 78.7% and 80%. HPP also has a small studio segment, which generated revenue of $31.17 million during the second quarter, roughly 18% of total HPP second-quarter revenue of $190 million. The studio is experiencing extremely elevated vacancy rates, with the in-service stage percentage leased at 63.6% dropping from 78.1% a year ago.

Debt Maturities And Liquidity

Total revenue of $190 million was down 12.8% over its year-ago comp and a miss by $9.56 million on consensus estimates. Funds from operations ("FFO") came in at $8 million, around $0.04 per share, and down from $24.5 million or $0.17 per share a year ago. HPP needs to throw as much liquidity as it can towards addressing its debt maturities. Total liquidity at the end of the second quarter of $1 billion was comprised mainly of HPP's unsecured revolving credit facility and $236 million of unrestricted cash and cash equivalents. There is roughly $172.87 million of debt set for repayment through the remainder of 2025, with $530.77 million coming due next year.

The Preferreds

HPP does not pay out a cash dividend to its common shareholders, suspending payments a year ago, two months before I last covered the ticker. This means the 4.750% Series C Cumulative Preferreds Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc. 4.750% CUM PFD C (NYSE:HPP.PR.C) are the only way to earn an income from the REIT. The Series C preferreds pay out a $1.1875 annual coupon for a current yield of 8%. This provides a 390 basis points spread over the United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) and is understandably a higher current yield than the preferreds of office REITs with stronger fundamentals. Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) Series O preferreds, Vornado Realty Trust 4.45% CUM PFD O (VNO.PR.O) have a current yield of 7.3%.

The credit rating, or Issuer Default Rating, for VNO was affirmed by Fitch Ratings at "BB+" a couple of weeks ago. This is a notch below investment grade. HPP most recently received a credit rating of "B+", a downgrade from a prior rating of "BB-". Fitch highlighted elevated leverage, cash flow volatility, and elevated occupancy levels as drivers for the downgrade. The preferreds are trading at a 41% discount to their $25 per share liquidation value, or for 59 cents on the dollar. The discount has tightened from the summer when they were trading just north of 50%.

Conclusion

HPP's wall of lease expirations is significant against an existing 3.98 million square feet of vacant office space as of the end of the second quarter. This huge volume of additional space set to expire over the next three years keeps me on the sidelines. Hence, even though the preferreds look set to be backstopped by decent liquidity and a debt maturity schedule that's a lot less front-loaded than expiring leases, they're simply not as attractive as other REIT preferreds. I'm rating both the commons and preferreds as a hold.

data.title: uniQure: Positive 3-Year AMT-130 Data Leads To Pre-BLA FDA Meeting And Beyond
data.description:
The last time I wrote an article on uniQure N.V. (NASDAQ:QURE), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "uniQure: AMT-130 For HD Progresses Forward With Q2 2025 FDA Meeting." With respect to that article, I laid out the nature of the company setting to meet with the FDA in a Type B meeting to discuss the potential registration pathway of its one-time gene therapy AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with Huntington's Disease [HD]. This meeting ended up with a positive outcome because of two reasons.

The first reason is because there was alignment with the FDA in terms of a statistical analysis plan [SAP] and chemistry, manufacturing, and controls [CMC] to support a Biologics License Application [BLA] of AMT-130 for the treatment of these patients with HD. The second reason is because the meeting ended up with a conclusion that the primary efficacy endpoint needed to support a BLA submission would be a three-year change in the composite Unified Huntington’s Disease Rating Scale [cUHDRS] compared to that of matched external control.

This second item is very important with respect to today's news, whereby it was announced that the high dose of AMT-130 achieved this primary endpoint of three-year change in cUHDRS in a statistically significant manner compared to this propensity score-matched external control. This released data set from this pivotal phase 1/2 study, using this gene therapy to treat these patients with this neurodegenerative disorder, caused the stock price to trade higher. As of this writing, the stock is trading higher by 243.12% at $46.87 per share. If such gains have been made, then why do I believe there might still be upside with respect to an investment here? It is because there are several other catalysts approaching that could yield further value.

For instance, this positive three-year data attained by the company is enough for it to now hold a pre-BLA meeting with the FDA, expected to happen in the 2nd half of 2025. From there, if this meeting goes as well as intended, it would allow uniQure to be in a position to file a BLA of AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with HD in Q1 of 2026. Lastly, there might be further value to be unlocked here, because not only does it intend to submit the BLA of this one-time gene therapy for these patients, but it also expects to request for Priority Review Designation. With the primary endpoint of three-year change in cUHDRS being met in the pivotal phase 1/2 study of AMT-130 for patients with HD, plus several other catalysts expected within a 1-year period, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Positive Three-Year AMT-130 Data For Huntington's Disease Leads To Potential First Disease-Modifying Therapy

As I noted above, uniQure achieved positive results from its phase 1/2 study using one-time gene therapy AMT-130 to treat patients with Huntington's Disease. As I will cite below, the primary efficacy endpoint being met in this pivotal trial leads to several other catalysts for investors to look forward to. Before going over this phase 1/2 study in detail, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity it could end up being for the company.

Huntington's Disease [HD] is characterized as a mutation of the HTT gene that causes the buildup of an abnormal protein known as huntingtin in the brain. A CAG repeat of the first exon of this gene [HTT] leads to a major problem that is not supposed to happen, and this is an expanded repeat beyond 35 repeats. This is what then allows the production of a toxic fragment to build up on the brain. The thing is that a normal person [one without this disorder] has a repeat of less than 35. This toxic protein buildup is what leads to these patients having symptoms and a decreased quality of life [QoL].

Okay, so how does AMT-130 help these patients? It is an adeno-associated virus [AAV] gene therapy with the AAV5 vector in place to silence the mRNA mHTT gene. This is done to reduce the buildup of toxic mHTT proteins in the brain. If not taken care of, neurons in the brain are damaged, and it could also lead to the disruption of balance of the basal ganglia, which is responsible for the following functions:

  • Cognitive functioning.
  • Motor movement.
  • Behavior.

Thus, with AMT-130 being given to these patients, their toxic buildup of mHTT protein buildup is reduced, and it is what could potentially lead to slowing of disease progression and then, in tandem, an improved QoL. Speaking of slowing of disease progression, this is what I'm going to go over before that was achieved in the phase 1/2 pivotal study of this one-time gene therapy. The bottom line is that AMT-130 can potentially become the first disease-modifying therapy for these HD patients. The global Huntington's disease treatment market is expected to reach $4.27 billion by 2033. This is a very large market opportunity, and with no approved disease-modifying therapy in place, this leaves this market wide open for the company.

In order to determine if AMT-130 as a one-time gene therapy would be able to be successfully used to treat patients with HD, the company deployed the pivotal phase 1/2 study for this program. This study was set up in such a way as to have two different dosing types tested versus a matched external control as follows:

  • High-dose AMT-130 gene therapy of 17 patients versus matched external control arm ENROLL-HD with 940 patients.
  • Low-dose AMT-130 gene therapy of 12 patients versus matched external control arm ENROLL-HD with 626 patients.

The primary endpoint deployed is the three-year change of the composite Unified Huntington’s Disease Rating Scale [cUHDRS] between AMT-130 versus matched external control. The final outcome noted was only in favor of the high dose of this gene therapy, whereby patients achieved a statistically significant 75% slowing of disease progression. This resulted in a statistically significant p-value of p=0.003. This is a devastating disorder, and even just the slowing of disease progression is a good thing. Patients taking AMT-130 still progressed in disease, but at a much slower rate than their counterparts in the external control group. To further illustrate this, these are the numbers to focus on:

  • AMT-130 high-dose patients only saw a loss of -0.38 points on this scale.
  • Matched external control patients [940 for high-dose] saw a loss of -1.52 points.

Again, patients still saw disease progression on high-dose AMT-130, but not by much. In addition, it was able to slow the rate of disease progression considerably by 75%. The cUHDRS measures 4 items of importance, which are Total Functional Capacity, Total Motor Score, Symbol Digit Modalities Test, and Stroop Word Reading. In essence, these deal with daily task completions, motor functioning, cognitive impairment, and tests of cognitive interference, respectively. If this wasn't enough, there was another positive. Although secondary endpoints are typically not needed for regulatory approvals, they are still assessed in clinical trials to determine if the therapy works with respect to other measures. With that being said, total function capacity [TFC] was also met in a statistically significant manner with a 60% slowing of disease progression. Again, this measures the ability for a person to form daily tasks.

The stock price did increase significantly on the back of this data release, but it is important to highlight that there are still other milestones that are expected from this program. The first of which is that uniQure is now set to hold a pre-BLA meeting with the FDA in the 2nd half of 2025. The importance of this is to confirm what was already established in the prior Type B meeting. Speaking of which, after this specific meeting, there was an alignment on SAP and CMC requirements in order to support a BLA filing of AMT-130. Especially the ever-important primary efficacy endpoint that was confirmed at that time, which would be the three-year change of cUHDRS at 36 months.

The most important takeaway from this is that this would confirm a plan for the company to be able to file for Accelerated Approval of this one-time AAV gene therapy. If the pre-BLA meeting goes well, then this brings about another major inflection point for it, which would be the actual filing of the BLA of AMT-130 itself in Q1 of 2026. It is bullish in and of itself to receive Accelerated Approval, but there is another possible outcome scenario that might present itself from the expected pre-BLA meeting. This would be in terms of the company wanting to request for Priority Review Designation of AMT-130 to treat these HD patients. Although such a request to cut down the review time of this gene therapy is not guaranteed to happen.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, uniQure had cash, cash equivalents, and current investment securities of $377 million as of June 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand was thanks to a follow-on offering that was completed in January and February of 2025 for net proceeds of $80.5 million. The company noted that it would have cash on hand for an extended period of time. That is, it had enough cash to fund its operations into the 2nd half of 2027. However, it seems that management wanted to gain further financial flexibility, and thus, it ended up with a structured $175 million non-dilutive senior secured term loan facility with Hercules Capital.
